abridged.title: Abridged Snapshot
abridged.content:
New-onset post-operative atrial fibrillation

Post-operative atrial fibrillation is common after cardiac surgery and matters because it can reduce cardiac output, increase myocardial oxygen demand, worsen hypotension, promote thromboembolism, and delay recovery. The loss of atrial contraction is particularly important in stiff ventricles, LV hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, RV dysfunction, mitral valve disease and patients dependent on AV synchrony.

Atrial fibrillation — An irregularly irregular atrial rhythm with no consistent P waves and chaotic atrial electrical activity, producing irregular ventricular response through the AV node.

Atrial kick — The late diastolic contribution of atrial contraction to ventricular filling. It may contribute 15–30 percent of ventricular filling and can be more important in stiff or hypertrophied ventricles.

AF presentation Likely impact Immediate response
AF rate 90–110/min, stable MAP, warm, good UO May be tolerated. Check K/Mg, oxygenation, pain, fluid status, review anticoagulation and rate/rhythm plan.
AF rate 130–160/min with hypotension Reduced filling time and loss of atrial kick causing low cardiac output. Escalate; prepare rate/rhythm control and possible synchronised cardioversion if unstable.
AF after valve surgery with rising CVP/low MAP May unmask RV dysfunction or diastolic dependence. Assess filling, echo need, pacing/AV synchrony, inotropes and surgical factors.
AF with chest pain/ST change Possible ischaemia trigger or consequence. 12-lead ECG, senior review, correct oxygen delivery and electrolytes.

Immediate escalation for AF with shock, severe hypotension, acute pulmonary oedema, ongoing ischaemia, altered consciousness, or rapid ventricular response causing low cardiac output. Prepare synchronised cardioversion pathway if unstable.

expanded.title: Expanded Deep-Dive
expanded.content:
Mechanism in cardiac surgery

Post-operative AF is driven by a combination of atrial inflammation, surgical manipulation, pericardial irritation, catecholamines, oxidative stress, electrolyte disturbance, fluid shifts, hypoxia, pain, infection, sleep disruption and autonomic imbalance. It often occurs around post-operative days 2–4, but it can occur immediately in ICU, especially after valve, atrial, aortic and complex surgery.

The haemodynamic effect depends on ventricular rate, ventricular function, filling dependence and valve pathology. In AF, atrial contraction is lost and ventricular filling becomes irregular. A patient with normal ventricles may tolerate this. A patient with LV hypertrophy, mitral stenosis/repair, RV dysfunction, pulmonary hypertension, diastolic dysfunction or low cardiac output syndrome may deteriorate rapidly because atrial contribution and AV timing are essential to filling.

Rapid ventricular response shortens diastole. This reduces ventricular filling time and coronary perfusion time while increasing myocardial oxygen demand. In a post-CABG or post-valve patient with stunned myocardium, tachycardic AF can create a spiral: less filling, lower stroke volume, lower MAP, poorer coronary perfusion, more ischaemia, more catecholamine release and faster AF.

Management concepts

Management is usually framed as rate control, rhythm control, correction of triggers, anticoagulation assessment and haemodynamic support. Rate control may involve beta-blockers, amiodarone or other agents depending on blood pressure and ventricular function. Rhythm control is more likely to be favoured when AF causes haemodynamic compromise, when onset is recent, when atrial kick is crucial, or when surgical teams prefer restoration of sinus rhythm. Anticoagulation decisions after cardiac surgery are complex because stroke prevention must be balanced against mediastinal bleeding, chest drains, pacing wires and re-operation risk.

pearls.title: Clinical Pearls
pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls

The same AF rate can be benign in one patient and catastrophic in another. The deciding factor is perfusion and dependence on atrial kick.

Post-op AF is often a symptom of something else: pain, atelectasis, low potassium, low magnesium, fluid overload, bleeding, infection or ischaemia.

In a stiff LV, mitral valve patient or hypertrophied ventricle, loss of atrial kick can cause hypotension even without a very fast rate.

Do not give rate-control drugs blindly to a patient whose AF is compensating for low stroke volume or shock. Assess perfusion and discuss early.

Atrial pacing can sometimes suppress atrial ectopy or support rate in selected post-op patients, but it requires functioning atrial wires and a clear plan.

equipment.title: Equipment Tie-In
equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In

Monitoring

Use ECG leads that show atrial activity and R-R irregularity clearly. Monitor rate trends, not just a single number, because AF ventricular response can vary with pain, movement, catecholamines and drugs. Obtain a 12-lead ECG to identify ischaemia, flutter, pre-excitation, paced rhythm or conduction disease.

Synchronised cardioversion setup

Equipment issue Risk Action
Sync markers not visible or marking T waves Unsafe shock timing and R-on-T risk. Do not shock until sensing is corrected or senior directs emergency defibrillation for arrest.
Monitor reads regular tachycardia AF/flutter/SVT misclassification. Print rhythm strip and obtain 12-lead.
Poor electrode contact False irregularity or missed AF. Replace electrodes and reduce artefact.
Pacing spikes present Device may misread QRS. Check sync markers carefully and involve senior staff.

targets.title: Targets and Numbers
targets.content:
Targets and Numbers

Parameter Usual target / threshold CT-ICU interpretation
AF ventricular rate Stable rate-control targets often <100–110/min; stricter or rhythm control may be needed if low output. Rate target is meaningless if MAP, lactate, UO or oxygenation are poor.
Unstable tachyarrhythmia Hypotension, shock, ischaemia, acute heart failure, altered consciousness. Synchronised cardioversion is time-critical.
Potassium Often target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L. Reduces atrial/ventricular ectopy risk.
Magnesium Often target ≥0.8–1.0 mmol/L. Replacement commonly used in post-op AF prevention/treatment.
QTc if amiodarone/other QT drugs Concern often >500 ms or rapid increase from baseline. Monitor electrolytes, bradycardia and concomitant QT-prolonging drugs.
MAP Usually 65–80 mmHg after routine CABG/valve surgery; often 70–90 mmHg after complex aortic surgery or chronic hypertension if ordered. Treat the number in context: lactate, urine output, mentation/sedation, skin perfusion, SvO2/ScvO2, echo and surgical goals.
Urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; commonly ≥30 mL/hr in average adult; rising concern if <0.3 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours. Low UO with hypotension and narrow pulse pressure suggests low flow until proven otherwise.
Lactate Usually expected to trend down; concern if >2 mmol/L and rising; high concern if ≥4 mmol/L or rising despite support. After CPB, lactate may be elevated, but persistent rise suggests oxygen debt, shock, bleeding, tamponade, severe vasoplegia or poor cardiac output.
Potassium Common rhythm target 4.0–4.5 mmol/L in CTICU unless renal failure/hyperkalaemia risk. Low K increases ectopy, AF, VT risk and digoxin sensitivity.
Magnesium Common rhythm target ≥0.8 mmol/L; many CTICUs target 1.0 mmol/L in AF/VT risk. Low Mg promotes ventricular ectopy and AF; replacement is common post bypass.

quiz.question: What ECG feature best supports atrial fibrillation?
options: Irregularly irregular rhythm with no consistent P waves
Regular narrow rhythm with sawtooth flutter waves
ST elevation in two contiguous leads
quiz.explanation: AF is characterised by absent consistent P waves and irregularly irregular ventricular response.

quiz.question: Why may AF cause hypotension in a post-mitral valve or hypertrophied LV patient even at a moderate rate?
options: Loss of atrial kick impairs ventricular filling
AF always causes complete heart block
quiz.explanation: Stiff ventricles and mitral pathology may rely heavily on atrial contraction for filling. Loss of atrial kick can reduce stroke volume.

quiz.question: Before synchronised cardioversion for unstable AF, what must be confirmed on the defibrillator?
options: Sync markers correctly identify QRS complexes
The oxygen saturation pleth is visible
The NIBP cuff is cycling every hour
quiz.explanation: Synchronised shock must be timed to the QRS. Incorrect sensing risks shock delivery during repolarisation.
